title: What an Embedding Actually Encodes
concept: A dense vector as a learned coordinate in meaning-space where geometric distance approximates semantic distance.

exercises.prompt: In previous lessons, we touched on how LLMs represent data. What is the basic idea behind an embedding?

options.text: Representing concepts as lists of numbers in a coordinate space
Translating words into different human languages automatically
Storing words in an alphabetical database for quick lookups

exercises.explain: Embeddings represent words as lists of numbers (vectors) so that computers can perform mathematical operations on semantic concepts.

cards.title: Mapping Meanings
cards.body: We know computers love numbers, but how do they grasp the subtle difference between 'stormy weather' and 'rainy day'? They don't read definitions; instead, they map words to coordinates in a giant, invisible map of meanings.

cards.title: The Meaning Coordinate
cards.body: An embedding is a dense vector—a list of numbers—that acts as a coordinate in a multi-dimensional 'meaning-space'. Words or sentences with similar meanings are placed close to each other, meaning their geometric distance is very small.

cards.title: The Infinite Library Grid
cards.body: Think of a giant library where books aren't sorted by author name, but by their vibe, topics, and tone. If you place books on a massive 3D grid, books about space travel sit close together in one corner, while cookbook recipes sit in another. The coordinates of each book's position are its embedding.

cards.title: Puppies and Skyscrapers
cards.body: If we embed the words 'puppy', 'dog', and 'skyscraper', the coordinates for 'puppy' and 'dog' will be extremely close to each other. The coordinate for 'skyscraper' will be parked far away in a completely different direction.

cards.title: No Human Labels Inside
cards.body: You might think each number in a 512-dimension embedding stands for a clear concept like 'is it red?' or 'is it alive?'. In reality, these dimensions are learned by AI and don't map to simple human labels; they work together to capture fuzzy concepts.

cards.title: Visualizing the Coordinates
cards.body: By looking at the coordinates, we can tell instantly how similar two concepts are. Close coordinates mean close concepts.

right.text: Sports Car: [-0.45, 0.78] \n(Far away in coordinate space!)

exercises.prompt: What does a dense vector embedding actually look like to a computer?

options.text: A single integer showing the word's rank in the dictionary
A string of binary code representing the characters of the word
A list of numbers representing coordinates in a high-dimensional space
A list of all synonyms for that word

exercises.explain: An embedding is represented as a dense vector, which is a list of decimal numbers (coordinates) representing a point in a high-dimensional space.

exercises.prompt: True or False: The goal of an embedding model is to make sure semantic distance (meaning) matches geometric distance (space).
exercises.explain: Semantic distance refers to how similar two concepts are in meaning, while geometric distance is the physical or mathematical space between their vector coordinates. We want them to match.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing phrase to complete the rule of embedding spaces:
exercises.sentence: In a semantic vector space, geometric distance is used to approximate how closely related two concepts are.
exercises.explain: In embedding spaces, we use geometric distance (like Euclidean distance or cosine similarity) to calculate how similar two concepts are.

exercises.prompt: Identify the AI-like approach to determining if the words 'king' and 'queen' are related.

options.text: We count the letters in both words and subtract the smaller number from the larger number.
We convert both words into dense vectors and measure the geometric distance between their coordinates.
We sort the words alphabetically and measure how many pages apart they are in a standard dictionary.

exercises.explain: The second option accurately describes the core principle of embeddings: using geometric distance in a learned vector space to measure semantic similarity. Option 1 describes simple keyword matching, and Option 3 describes alphabetical sorting.

exercises.prompt: Let's pull it all together. When we convert words into a dense vector embedding, what is the core relationship we are trying to capture in the vector space?

options.text: Geometric distance between points corresponds to the semantic similarity between the concepts.
The numbers represent the exact character counts and alphabetical sorting of the input words.
Each dimension corresponds to a human-labeled dictionary category like 'noun' or 'verb'.
The vectors are randomly generated and have no relationship to the meaning of the words.

exercises.explain: Embeddings are learned coordinates in a high-dimensional space. The distance between these coordinates (geometric distance) is mathematically designed to match how similar the concepts are in the real world (semantic distance).
